title: The Macro Minute: Should investors chase or fade rallies?

Happy Wednesday out there, team 42. Your
skipper here, Darius Dale, with our
macro minute for Wednesday, March 25th,
2026. Hope everyone's having a great
week. As always, we start with the
executive summary from today's Lead Off
Morning Note. So, let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, should
investors chase or fade rallies? The
answer is, traders can continue to have
an action bias sponsored party, but
investors should continue to beware of
temporary reprieve in this conflict.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis, starting with
the growth cycle. The US is preliminary
March composite PMI data did not support
our paradigm C theme. Recall that
paradigm C is the grow phase of the cut
grow print sequence required to address
the geopolitically driven supply demand
imbalance in the Treasury bond market,
which has been our core research view
since the summer of 2023.
The S&P Global PMIs have been a leading
indicator for the ISM PMIs in recent
years and signal a slow down in the
months ahead.
Elsewhere, analysts have consistently
upgraded US earnings expectations
throughout the geopolitical conflict
with the S&P 500 index profits projected
to increase roughly 12% year-over-year
in Q1 and 20% in 2026 on a Q4 over Q4
basis.
This is consistent with our paradigm C
productivity boom, jobless recovery, and
cooling housing and labor themes. None
of these themes matter much as long as
the current global liquidity crisis
persists.
Traders can continue to have an action
bias sponsored party, but investors
should continue to beware of temporary
reprieve in this conflict because the
left tail of the distribution of
probable outcomes has yet to be
seriously entertained by equity and
credit markets because of how accurate
we've been regarding the aforementioned
fundamental themes, which are all
bullish. Again, the best Actually, let
me finish the statement before I go
there. Permanent control of the Strait
of Hormuz is far more is a far more
meaningful catalyst for asset markets
from here given the precondition of a
historic degree of crowded bullish
positioning. Recall that we entered this
year with our positioning model
signaling that investors consensus was
as lopsidedly bullish as they have been
in any market cycle dating back to 1987.
So, just summarizing that, investors
came into the year as lopsidedly bullish
as they've ever been, at least back to
over the last 40 years or so. And in our
view, the reason they were so lopsidedly
bullish is because we've been right in
forecasting all of this positive
economic development that is now showing
up in earnings expectations. But, as we
say in the in this in this call, you
have to beware given the precondition of
all that crowded bullish positioning and
the Bayesian prior amongst most
investors of all this positive stuff.
You know, the once the positive stuff
ends, that's how you unwind the
positioning cycle. And so, we're
concerned about that from a medium-term
perspective given what we're about to
talk about in the inflation cycle. So,
let's just pivot right to it. Iran
stated foreign vessels may transit the
Strait of Hormuz provided they are not
supporting aggression against Iran
and comply with Iran's new regulatory
framework. These remarks were delivered
in a letter distributed Tuesday to the
International Maritime Organization
numbers.
Despite Iran's new supreme leader leader
Mojtaba Khamenei previously advocating
closure of the strait,
the letter articulates a more refined
and conditional policy stance, likely
signaling Beijing's influence on the
developments. Most notably, Iranian
officials indicated oil prices will not
revert to prior levels until all threats
against the nation are eliminated. This
is exactly what we have been warning
about regarding Iran controlling the
marginal marginal supply of global
energy products in our prior two Lead
Off Morning Notes.
The US administration has taken many L's
in recent months according to a broad
swath of polling data and special
elections across the country, including
in Mar-a-Lago last night. Effectively
seeding control of global energy markets
to the world's largest state sponsor of
terrorism would be the biggest L of them
all,
which is precisely why we believe this
conflict is likely to drag on well
beyond Friday's day tons deadline.
Elsewhere, in the UK, the February CBI
and PBI data generally did not support
our outlook for slowing inflation. With
regards to the fiscal, our monetary
policy cycle, fiscal policy cycle, and
liquidity cycles, nothing actionable
with respect to our Bayesian priors.
Transitioning to the positioning cycle,
Iran dismissed a ceasefire overture and
labeled the US negotiations as quote
illogical for the semi-official Fars
News Agency. Recall that President Trump
recently confirmed that the US had been
negotiating with Iran regarding a
proposed 15-point framework encompassing
sanctions relief, a rollback of Iran's
nuclear programs program, and
constraints on missiles and unfettered
access to the Strait of Hormuz. Iran,
via its semi-official Fars outlet,
asserted that it will not accept the
ceasefire under current conditions and
deems such an arrangement as presently
infeasible. Moreover, Iran's forces
reinforced a steady stream of
communications rejecting ceasefire
negotiations according to the state-run
IRIB News. In a stronger show of
rejection, Iran continues strikes
targeting Arab Gulf states and Israel.
Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, Iran's speaker
of parliament, former Tehran mayor, and
ex-Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps
commander, has consolidated influence
within Iran's wartime leadership.
Ghalibaf's historical record and wartime
rhetoric suggests Iran will remain
resistant to US demands.
He is known for being a staunch defender
of Iran's nearly 50-year-old theocratic
system and has recently implied Iran is
prevailing by disrupting global energy
markets and exerting economic pressure
on President Trump.
Perhaps more importantly, Ghalibaf is a
close friend of Iran's new supreme
leader Mojtaba Khamenei. Their
friendship dates extends back to the
Iran-Iraq War, which ended nearly 40
years ago.
Investors must prepare for both sides of
this geopolitical conflict to dig in.
So, as always, continue reviewing to
learn whether our institutional grade
risk management overlays, KISS and
Doctor Mo, are evolving their signals in
response to these or anticipated
fundamental dynamics. As always,
transitioning to our 42 macro dashboard
here. As always, we wrap up with a
question from our community. This one's
called titled the Hormuz tax. It says,
"Good morning, Darius. A quick question.
If Iran gets away with just taxing the
Strait of Hormuz, isn't that a tolerable
outcome for asset markets? It's just one
more thing to plan and invest around,
mundane even,
from finally
finally defrosting Helsinki." They're
right now, New York's barely defrosting
as well. So, cheers to you, my friend.
So, yes, I think this is a reasonable
palatable outcome for asset markets,
particularly in the context of those
positive fundamental themes we've been
accurate on, paradigm C. The the the the
the run it hot theme, which we pivoted
to back in April of last year when
everybody was freaking out about
tariffs. Productivity boom,
jobless recovery, cooling housing and
labor. These are all things that should
perpetuate a faster growth rate of
corporate profits. And so, in our view,
we think the markets can generally trade
well if the result of this conflict is,
okay, Iran's just, you know, charging a
tax on ships going through the Strait of
Hormuz. The issue, the bigger issue in
my perspective, is the global liquidity
crisis that we've been talking about
over the past few days or perhaps week
or so.
And then, the reason I bring that up is
because again, you think about the
barrels of crude oil that generate
revenues, you know, for for those
regions. If the war goes on and those
barrels do not get on boats and generate
revenues for the Gulf Coast economies in
those regions, then those sovereign
wealth funds, which are some of the
biggest sovereign wealth funds in the
rest of the in the world, won't be able
to to hit the bid on global capital
markets like they've consistently done
over the years. Moreover, the economies
that need those barrels of crude oil,
particularly in Japan, and China,
Taiwan, and Hong Kong, Singapore, you
know, Indonesia, these economies, India,
all these Asian economies that need
these barrels of crude oil to generate
production won't be able to generate the
profits, the household savings, and the
foreign exchange reserves that they have
historically used to, you know, backstop
global capital markets. So, you know,
this is a liquidity crisis as we talked
about yesterday
in yesterday's macro minute with Turkey
arranging for, you know, large-scale
sales of its
of gold reserves, you know, this is the
kind of thing that if those barrels of
crude oil are removed from the market,
we have big problems. If the barrels of
crude oil are on the market, no matter
what price they are, we're fine. I think
the markets can generally look through a
Hormuz tax. But to me, it's not about
the tax, it's about whether or not the
marginal supply is being restricted at
all. You know, there's a there's a
chance that Iran could, you know, decide
to say, "Okay, if you're a friend of
ours, we will ship you barrels of crude
oil. If you're not a friend
[clears throat] of ours, we won't allow
you to ship barrels of crude oil, and we
won't allow you to receive those barrels
of crude oil."
I that's an incredibly untenable outcome
from the perspective of the
administration, and so I doubt that is
the ultimate end here, but getting to
something that would be a tenable
outcome if that's the position that
Ghalibaf and and and Khamenei decide to
take.
It's going to require much more
prolonged conflict in in our opinion.
We don't think markets are effectively
pricing that in yet, particularly in the
equity and credit markets. So, wrapping
up, we'll just wrap up with a comment
from
one of our one of the members of our
global investor community. This one's
titled Looking Back on a Year in KISS.
It says, "DD, I've been a 42 macro
subscriber for about a year, and it's
been an incredible experience. Before
Liberation Day, I had spent years
sitting in cash, influenced by the
endless stream of YouTube bears calling
for the next imminent and always
plausible market crash. I had even seen
you on Thoughtful Money and remember
wondering why you were so bullish." I
felt like a kid trying to jump into the
game of double Dutch. I just couldn't
find my way back into the market, which
is very consistent. Our friend Tom Keene
over Bloomberg, which I'll be on next
month, he consistently talks about this
kind of go to cash and stay at cash
mentality that a lot of folks in his
generation and the generation after that
have been forced to learn this bad habit
they've been forced to learn as a
function of the global financial crisis
and the dot-com bust. Getting back into
the comment,
then Liberation Day hit. While people
were panicking, I was buying VOO. A few
days later cinema shifted, I sold for
quick profit and I found myself asking,
now what? I had decent instincts but no
real framework. That was the moment I
decided to take some of the money I made
on the trade and buy what you call
institutional grade risk management. I
needed guidance on when when to be in,
when to be out, and how not to white
knuckle every market move. I joined 42
macro and implemented KISS right away.
Since then my portfolio has grown more
than 20%. I'm not suddenly on the verge
of retirement. I'm in my 40s with young
kids, but I truly feel confident I can
get there on time, maybe even enjoy the
ride along the way. Right there with
you. So
so I appreciate you being side by side
with you on this journey. I'll see you
in the Mediterranean in 20 years.
As a year plus subscriber, sure I can
look back and wish I had top tick gold.
That's just human nature, but if on the
day on the day I implemented KISS, you
had shown me where I'd eventually be
exiting gold, I would have never
believed it. That whole experience is
exactly what a positively skew return
distribution feels like in real life.
I'll leave you with this. I didn't go to
college, I worked my way up in
construction, built a decent career. I
also didn't learn much about money with
from my family, and I did learn one
thing from my dad. Save diligently, live
below your means, and let time work for
you. He's a state worker who saved his
way into a sizable nest egg by being
basically naked long the S&P 500 for
decades.
So appreciate this this kind feedback.
You know, this is exactly why we built
the company.
You know, we're here to eradicate, not
just narrow.
Not just narrow, completely eradicate
the performance,
the habits,
and the
you know, lifestyle asymmetry between
you know, the folks we serve across the
global buy side, the institutional
investors we serve, and who partner with
us and partner with me personally for
years, for almost two decades. We want
to eradicate that asymmetry between what
they're experiencing from a lifestyle,
performance, habits perspective, and
what the general investing public feels.
So it always warms my heart when I find

regular people, people who grew up, you
know, maybe not like me. I had a very
extreme

to compound in the good times, offering
protection when conditions turn against
you. Thanks for all that you do. So
yeah.
This is
very thoughtful construction worker
telling you that KISS is doing exactly
what we've been telling you KISS has
been doing for for years, which is it
creates a positively skewed return
distribution in your portfolios, which I
would argue most retail investors don't
have the ability to do because they're
not using institutional grade risk
management. This is what institutions
get paid to do for their clients. That's
why they charge two and 20, or the folks
who I mimic when we built when we
designed the KISS and the Doctrines of
Macro systems, they charge three and 30
to their customers and by and by and
large you can't become a customer there
is because they generally have gates not
gates, but they generally don't want
more money. They have too much money.
You know, that's why they're able to
charge three and 30, five and 50 for
certain funds of theirs. And so we try
to mimic what we think is the best of
across the global buy side from a macro
risk management perspective, and from a
portfolio risk management perspective,
particularly from the lens of a
portfolio construction, asset
allocation, market timing. And those
types of systems, particularly
volatility targeting and dynamic
position sizing all being generated by
systematic quantitative overlays, those
are the systems that we use in KISS to
create outcomes like this.
And so if you want outcomes like this,
come join us. If you want to keep doing
what you're doing out there, we're happy
to support you too. I just want to say
thank you for putting up with these
macro minutes. I know they've been long
in recent months, but look, there's a

if you want to play the game at the

we're doing. So everyone have a
wonderful day.
